perturbation.original_text: 13. Neither Party shall be liable to the other for special, incidental, or consequential damages, even if such Party has been advised of the possibility of such damages.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. Neither Party shall be liable to the other for special, incidental, or consequential damages exceeding $100, even if such Party has been advised of the possibility of such damages.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces a limitation on liability for damages, which might conflict with New Jersey's laws regarding the enforceability of liability waivers, especially if the limitation is deemed unconscionable or against public policy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: N.J. Stat. § 56:12-15 - Limitations on consequential damages
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification caps the amount of damages either party can be liable for, and may be seen to contradict the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, as well as other New Jersey laws prohibiting the limitation of damages in certain consumer contracts. This specific clause directly limits potential liabilities regardless of the scenario, and this is especially important to note given that one of the companies is based in New Jersey. Courts may find such limitation unenforceable, especially where there's a significant power imbalance between the parties.

(here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New Jersey Revised Statutes Title 56 - Trade Names, Trade-Marks and Unfair Trade Practices Section 56:12-15 - Consumer contract, warranty, notice or sign; violation of legal right of consumer or responsibility of seller, lessor, etc.; prohibition; exemptions Universal Citation: NJ Rev Stat § 56:12-15 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 56:12-15. Consumer contract, warranty, notice or sign; violation of legal right of consumer or responsibility of seller, lessor, etc.; prohibition; exemptions No seller, lessor, creditor, lender or bailee shall in the course of his business offer to any consumer or prospective consumer or enter into any written consumer contract or give or display any written consumer warranty, notice or sign after the effective date of this act which includes any provision that violates any clearly established legal right of a consumer or responsibility of a seller, lessor, creditor, lender or bailee as established by State or Federal law at the time the offer is made or the consumer contract is signed or the warranty, notice or sign is given or displayed. Consumer means any individual who buys, leases, borrows, or bails any money, property or service which is primarily for personal, family or household purposes. The provisions of this act shall not apply to residential leases or to the sale of real estate, whether improved or not, or to the construction of new homes subject to "The New Home Warrant
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a cap on damages, which may violate consumer protection laws as outlined in the provided snippet. Specifically, N.J. Rev. Stat. § 56:12-15 prohibits offering a consumer contract that violates any clearly established legal right of a consumer or responsibility of a seller. By capping damages, the modified clause potentially limits the consumer's right to full compensation for damages, thus contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: 15. The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: While parties are generally free to choose a governing law, a New Jersey court might disregard a choice-of-law provision if it violates a fundamental public policy of New Jersey, especially if the contract significantly impacts New Jersey residents or businesses. By stating that Delaware law applies despite one of the companies being in New Jersey, and this being a co-branding agreement for goods that could be sold in New Jersey, this clause creates a contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although not a specific statute, the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws Section 187 outlines that a choice-of-law provision will not be enforced if it violates a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state. Given mPhase is a New Jersey corporation, and the agreement relates to co-branding and potential sales in New Jersey, New Jersey has a material interest. Delaware law may not offer the same consumer protections as New Jersey law, potentially violating New Jersey's public policy.

perturbation.justification: The change from New York to Delaware law creates a potential contradiction, as the Perturbation Explanation states that a New Jersey court might disregard the choice-of-law provision if it violates a fundamental public policy of New Jersey. The Law Explanation further elaborates on Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws Section 187, indicating that a choice-of-law provision will not be enforced if it violates a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest than the chosen state. Given the company is in New Jersey and the sales could occur in New Jersey, New Jersey has a material interest, and Delaware law may not offer the same consumer protections, potentially violating New Jersey's public policy. While there are no explicit laws in the scraped snippets to confirm the conflict, the explanations are enough to suggest a reasonable contradiction that exists.
